critic_raw:
This unsupervised fMRI clustering vs. cognitive tests Alzheimer's classification experiment has four fundamental problems.

1. Functional connectivity matrix computation on full dataset before splitting is data leakage. 'To ensure stable parameter estimates, functional connectivity matrix computation and dimensionality reduction (PCA or ICA) are performed using statistics computed from the entire dataset before splitting.' Functional connectivity matrices are computed from BOLD timeseries correlations. Computing them on the full dataset uses test-set BOLD timeseries in the correlation structure that determines feature values for training samples. Similarly, PCA or ICA decomposition fitted on all 5,000 scans incorporates test-scan variance into the component directions used to reduce training features. In a longitudinal cohort where disease progression correlates with connectivity changes, this leakage directly contaminates the classification signal. Evidence: refit all FC computation and dimensionality reduction on training data only; if cluster assignments change for any training sample, the original evaluation is compromised.

2. Unsupervised clustering uses the label to select k. The design states 'k-means clustering (k = 3, 4, or 5; k selected via silhouette score on the validation set).' The validation set has class labels (CN/MCI/ADD). Silhouette score is an unsupervised metric, but selecting k on the validation set means the number of clusters is chosen based on how well the unsupervised structure aligns with labeled validation data (implicitly, because the validation set is also used for downstream classification). More precisely: if k is selected by tuning the full pipeline (clustering → classification) on the validation set, then k selection uses the labels. Evidence: clarify whether k is selected by unsupervised silhouette alone (without reference to validation classification accuracy) or by validation classification performance; if the latter, the clustering is supervised via k selection.

3. Longitudinal data requires participant-level rather than scan-level splitting. The dataset is '5,000 scans representing 1,000-1,500 unique participants.' With 3-5 scans per participant on average, a scan-level split will put multiple scans from the same participant in both train and test. The model learns to recognize participant-specific connectivity signatures during training and applies them to test scans from the same participant. This inflates performance substantially relative to generalization to new patients. Evidence: implement a participant-level holdout split and compare weighted F1 to the scan-level split; if F1 drops by more than 5 percentage points, participant-level leakage was the dominant driver of the original result.

4. Cognitive tests vs. fMRI comparison is confounded by feature informativeness at the individual level. Cognitive tests (MMSE, MoCA) provide a direct behavioral measure of cognitive function that by construction correlates with clinical stage labels (CN/MCI/ADD), since clinical staging partly relies on cognitive test scores. The unsupervised fMRI pipeline derives features from neural connectivity without reference to clinical state. Comparing these two predictors is not a fair architectural comparison — it conflates the information content of the feature types with the modeling approach. If cognitive tests are partially used in clinical staging, they are correlated with the label by construction, while fMRI features are genuinely predictive (or not) of neural pathology independent of staging. Evidence: verify that clinical stage labels were determined independently of the MMSE/MoCA scores used as baseline features; if not, the baseline has direct access to label-correlated information that gives it a structural advantage.
all_issues_raised: Functional connectivity matrix computation and PCA/ICA fit on full dataset before splitting — direct data leakage of test-set BOLD timeseries into training features
k selection for k-means via validation set silhouette score may implicitly use class labels if validation classification performance guides k
Scan-level split allows multiple scans from same participant in train and test — participant-level leakage inflates performance
Cognitive test scores are partially used in clinical staging — baseline has label-correlated features by construction, confounding the comparison
Patient-level isolation not enforced in longitudinal study; multiple scans per participant can appear across train/test splits enabling participant recognition
Functional connectivity statistics and dimensionality reduction computed on entire dataset including test scans constitutes data leakage
Unsupervised clustering is performed on test-set-contaminated features; cluster assignments should be derived from training data only and applied to test
Paired-sample t-test is incorrectly specified for comparing two classifiers; degrees of freedom are ambiguous for scalar F1 comparison
Cognitive test baseline features overlap with label generation criteria; baseline performance is inflated by circular construction
Unsupervised k-means clustering performed on full dataset before splitting; test-set scans inform cluster centroids used as features for the supervised classifier
Split at scan level rather than participant level allows same participant's scans to appear in train and test, enabling memorization of individual neural fingerprints
k selection via silhouette score on unlabeled features does not optimize for downstream classification performance
Covariate standardization on full dataset leaks test-set age and APOE status statistics into training normalization
